summary: Raw early‑Dead energy with scorching Rider and Mindbender
blurb: The Avalon set bursts with blues‑rock vigor, highlighted by a fiery I Know You Rider, a soaring Mindbender, and an unforgettable Cream Puff War. Jerry’s lead work is on fire while Bill drives the groove on Viola Lee Blues and Good Lovin'.
review: On May 19, 1966 at San Francisco’s Avalon Ballroom the Grateful Dead delivered a raw, dance‑floor‑ready set that still feels fresh decades later. The first half rocks from Beat It On Down The Line into an incendiary I Know You Rider and a blistering Cream Puff War, while Jerry Garcia’s lead guitar swings between wailing blues licks and nervous early‑psychedelic vibrato on Mindbender. Phil Lesh lays down sweet, solid bass that underpins the band’s youthful freedom, even if his low end gets a little muddy at times. Pigpen steps forward with an organ solo on Good Lovin', giving a rare glimpse of his keyboard chops before he became a full‑time frontman. Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming is tight and propulsive, especially during Viola Lee Blues and the closing jam. The second set keeps the momentum with Sittin' On Top Of The World, New Minglewood Blues, and a heartfelt rendition of Silver Threads And Golden Needles, rounding out an early‑Dead performance that captures both garage‑rock grit and the first stirrings of psychedelia.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are clearly positive, praising standout tracks (Rider, Mindbender, Cream Puff War) and overall band fire; three recordings are mixed but still note strong moments rather than outright criticism. The consensus leans solidly positive, warranting a moderate +1.0 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Good‑quality soundboard tape with crisp separation, minimal dropouts, and the most complete set; captures the raw energy and standout songs clearly.

key_highlights: Scorching lead guitar on Rider and Mindbender
Pigpen’s rare early organ solo on Good Lovin'
Tight, propulsive drumming by Bill on Viola Lee Blues
Early psychedelic vibe that hints at the band’s future direction
song_highlights: Sittin' On Top Of The World

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers scorching lead work, wailing blues licks and an early psychedelic vibrato on Mindbender; his tone is praised as fiery though one reviewer called it immature.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides solid, sweet‑blues bass foundation; vocal vibrato on Mindbender noted positively despite occasional muddiness in the low end.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals are described as youthful and pre‑pubescent; no major instrumental highlights were noted.
band_performance.Pigpen: Shows a rare early organ solo on Good Lovin', adding a distinctive texture to the set.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann drives the groove with tight, propulsive drumming, especially on Viola Lee Blues and Cream Puff War; one tape’s noise‑reduction caused a backward‑sounding drum effect.

must_listen_sequences: Sittin' On Top Of The World
